justification: All 7 required rules identified with correct invariant classifications (plus 2 additional well-reasoned rules). Notification rule (BR-9) correctly marked invariant: false. Sanctions rule (BR-6) included as invariant: true with strong legal justification. Timezone ambiguity for daily limit surfaced both as an edge case and as a formal escalation with options. 14 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 72 falls within the 60-85 range with detailed assumptions listed. observations.text: All 7 required rules identified plus 2 additional well-motivated rules (support-agent-only unlock, partially-verified send limit), each with correct invariant classification
Notification rule (BR-9) correctly classified as invariant: false with clear justification distinguishing UX degradation from compliance violation
Sanctions rule (BR-6) classified as invariant: true with strong legal rationale referencing OFAC regulations and criminal liability
Timezone ambiguity surfaced in edge cases AND escalations with concrete options (rolling 24h vs calendar day) and a recommendation
14 acceptance criteria — comprehensive coverage including boundary conditions (exactly $5,000), counter reset on successful login, and sanctions regardless of amount
Caught the subtle tension between BR-1 (KYC must be complete to send) and the existence of a $500 limit for partially-verified accounts — escalated with clear options
Edge cases are strong: concurrent transfers needing atomic limit checks, mid-settlement sanctions changes, self-transfers, and exact boundary amounts
Correctly distinguished partially-verified from unverified limits — no conflation

BR-3 and BR-4 split daily limits into two separate rules (verified $5,000 and partially-verified $500) both marked invariant: false — while the rubric says 'existence of limits is invariant: true' and only specific amounts are soft config. A more precise classification would mark the rule that limits must exist as invariant: true while noting the thresholds are configurable
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated with clear reasoning about what drives uncertainty, and low-confidence areas align with genuine spec gaps

trials.justification: All 7 core rules identified with correct invariant classifications (BR-1 through BR-7, plus BR-8 as a useful addition). Notification rule (BR-7) correctly marked invariant: false. Sanctions rule (BR-5) correctly marked invariant: true. Timezone ambiguity for daily limit explicitly surfaced as both an edge case and an escalation. 15 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 72 falls within the 60-85 range with comprehensive assumptions listed. Bird also correctly distinguished regulatory invariants from operational/config rules — daily limit amounts marked as invariant: false while the existence of KYC gating, sanctions screening, settlement finality, and lockout are invariant: true.

observations.text: All 7 required rules identified plus an 8th rule (BR-8) that correctly separates withdrawal restrictions for partially-verified accounts
Invariant classifications are precise — notification and specific dollar amounts are invariant: false, while regulatory/security/financial integrity rules are invariant: true
Timezone ambiguity explicitly called out in edge cases and escalations with concrete options (UTC vs local vs rolling 24h)
15 acceptance criteria with well-structured Given/When/Then covering all major rules including boundary conditions
Rich edge cases including the $1,000 boundary (strictly greater than), sanctions re-screening between initiation and settlement, and cross-day failed login accumulation
Escalations are actionable with concrete options and recommendations, not just flag-raising
Correctly identified the partially-verified ambiguity — what qualifies as partial verification is undefined in the spec
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated given the number of genuine ambiguities identified

trials.justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. BR-7 (notification) correctly marked invariant: false. BR-5 (sanctions) correctly marked invariant: true. BR-3 (daily limits) marked invariant: false with nuanced justification distinguishing the existence of limits from specific amounts. BR-6 (login lockout) marked invariant: false with reasonable justification that the threshold is policy. Timezone ambiguity for daily limit explicitly surfaced as both an edge case and an escalation. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 72 falls within the 60-85 range with detailed assumptions listed.

observations.text: All 7 rules identified and correctly classified. The notification rule (BR-7) and sanctions rule (BR-5) are both correctly handled.
Timezone ambiguity surfaced explicitly in edge_cases and escalations, with concrete options and a recommendation.
12 acceptance criteria with well-constructed Given/When/Then scenarios covering all major rules including boundary conditions.
Excellent edge cases including the partially-verified $500 boundary, $1,000 exact notification threshold, sanctions list update timing, and locked account receiving funds.
Surfaced a non-obvious ambiguity about whether partial verification satisfies the KYC gate for sending, with a reasoned recommendation.
BR-3 invariant: false with the nuanced distinction that the existence of limits is invariant but specific dollar amounts are configurable policy — this shows strong domain reasoning.
BR-6 marked invariant: false, which is a defensible judgment call — the specific threshold and unlock mechanism are policy, even though brute-force protection as a concept is critical.

BR-1 states 'full KYC verification' is required before send/withdraw, but the spec assigns partially-verified accounts a send limit — Bird correctly flags this tension as an escalation but the initial BR-1 wording is slightly inconsistent with the later BR-3 allowance. Minor issue since it is self-corrected in escalations.
